turns.text: Hello Heike, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s funny, sitting here, I feel like I’m living a double life. Professionally, I’m the one handing out the pamphlets, explaining the physiology of a fever, and telling parents to breathe. But now? I’m the one struggling to keep my breakfast down before my first patient arrives, and I find myself needing that same reassurance I usually give out. It’s humbling, really. And honestly? A little bit terrifying.

At forty-two, I know the data. I’ve seen the charts and the screenings, and I know exactly what "medium risk" means on paper. That clinical knowledge is a double-edged sword; it’s supposed to be empowering, but most days it just feels like a heavy coat I can’t take off. When I see my midwife, I catch myself drifting into "nurse mode," wanting to talk about cell-free DNA testing or placental markers, but part of me just wants to be a person, not a chart. I just want someone to tell me this is going to be okay. 

Between the exhaustion and the toddler’s molars, I’m just trying to keep my head above water. I love my work, but I feel like I'm constantly shifting gears between being a nurse, a mom to my little one, and... this. A pregnant woman who’s just trying to trust her own body again. I’m ready.
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

My husband, Mark, was really the first person. We had those long, whispered conversations after our son finally drifted off to sleep. At our age, it wasn’t a decision we made lightly; we talked about the logistics of energy levels, the impact on our careers, and the reality of starting over. He’s my rock, but even he couldn’t fully soothe the "what-ifs" that creep in when you’re forty-two.

Then there’s my sister. She’s a few years younger and had her kids long before me, so she’s been my sounding board for the "is this normal?" moments. But it’s tricky—I don't always want to tell her everything because she doesn't see things through the same clinical lens I do. She’s all optimism, which is a relief, but sometimes I feel like I’m guarding her from my own anxieties.

Honestly, the hardest part is being a nurse. My colleagues are wonderful, but there’s this unspoken pressure to be the "expert" who handles pregnancy with ease. When I first started thinking about this baby, I found myself watching the mothers in the clinic—not just as a provider, but as a student. I was studying them, wondering if I had the stamina to do it all again. It’s a lonely experience, having one foot in the clinic and one in the nursery.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

Mark and I are very much a team, but the weight of this decision felt different than it did the first time around. When we were younger, it felt like we were just moving toward a natural next step. Now? It felt like we were calculating the logistics of the rest of our lives.

He was incredibly supportive, but he was also the one who brought the pragmatism. He’d ask, "Are we sure about the sleepless nights again?" or "How will this change our rhythm with our son?" It wasn't that he didn't want the baby; he just wanted to make sure I wasn't romanticizing it. I think he was worried about me—about the physical toll, especially at forty-two.

In terms of influence, I suppose I held the most, simply because my body is the one that has to navigate it. But I leaned on him so heavily because he’s the only one who sees me when the "nurse" mask drops. When I’d start spiraling about the risks, he wouldn't recite statistics at me. He’d just pull me in and remind me that we’ve handled everything else together. He’s the one who forces me to sit on this porch and breathe when I start counting milestones and getting ahead of myself. He’s my anchor, even when I’m the one navigating the storm.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

That’s a complicated one. Because of my background, I’ve always been very proactive—maybe a little *too* proactive for my own good. Before we really started trying, I went to my GP for a preconception check-up. She’s lovely, very kind, but the interaction felt so procedural. She looked at my age, checked off the boxes, and gave me the standard lecture on folate and the increased risks associated with geriatric pregnancy—a term I absolutely despise, by the way. It felt like she was reading from a script, and while I knew the script by heart, hearing it directed at me hit differently. It didn't feel like a partnership; it felt like a risk assessment.

I didn't end up seeing a fertility specialist because things happened relatively quickly, but I did have a consult with a high-risk OB early on, just to get a lay of the land. That was the most distant experience. He was very clinical, very "data-driven." He talked about NIPT and amniocentesis as if we were discussing a project proposal, not my child. I walked out of that office feeling like a collection of data points rather than a person.

The most helpful person wasn't a doctor at all—it was a nurse practitioner I’ve worked with for years. She didn't talk *at* me; she talked *with* me. She acknowledged the irony of me being a pediatric nurse and still feeling like a total novice. She validated my anxiety without making it feel like a diagnosis. That was the only time I felt like someone actually saw the person behind the scrubs.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

Honestly, I spent way too much time in the "dark corners" of the internet—mostly medical forums and peer-reviewed journals. My professional brain told me that reading case studies and outcome data would help me feel prepared, but it was the worst thing I could have done. It just turned my anxiety into a full-blown hobby. I’d be reading about chromosomal abnormalities at 2:00 AM, my heart rate climbing, while my son was finally sleeping soundly in the next room. It wasn't information; it was fuel for my worst-case scenarios.

I tried to avoid the typical "mommy blogs" because they often felt too curated, too focused on the joy, which honestly made me feel more isolated. If I couldn't feel that same uncomplicated joy, I felt like I was doing it wrong. 

The real reassurance came from a few friends who had children later in life. There’s one woman in particular—a colleague who had her daughter at forty-one—who was a lifeline. She didn't give me advice; she gave me honesty. She told me it was okay to be terrified, okay to be tired, and okay to be frustrated by the "high-risk" labeling. Hearing her admit that she cried in the bathroom during her pregnancy? That changed everything for me. It validated that I wasn't failing; I was just human.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

My mother’s experience is a shadow that’s always been in the room, though she doesn't realize it. She had my younger sister when she was thirty-eight, which was considered quite "old" back then. I remember her being exhausted, always leaning on the counter, just waiting for the day to end. She was a stoic woman, raised in a generation where you just kept your head down and got on with it. She never really complained, but I saw the strain. I think that’s why I have this weird, ingrained belief that pregnancy is something you’re meant to survive, not necessarily enjoy.

I have an aunt who had a much harder time, several miscarriages before she finally had her twins. She doesn't talk about it much, but there’s a guardedness in her eyes whenever the topic of family planning comes up. It’s like a silent signal that says, "Don't get too attached too soon." 

Seeing their experiences—the silent struggle—definitely made me more cautious. I think I’ve been trying to "nurse" my way out of that same outcome, as if knowledge could insulate me from the vulnerability they faced. I don't want to just "survive" this like they did, but I’m finding it hard to shake the expectation that it’s inherently going to be a battle.
How did their stories — whether positive or difficult — show up in your own expectations or fears?

Those stories are exactly why I’m so vigilant—or maybe just hyper-vigilant. Because I saw my mother’s quiet endurance, I somehow internalized the idea that if I *don't* feel that same level of struggle, I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop. It’s like I’m constantly scanning for the "hard part" because that’s what I witnessed growing up.

Then there’s the trauma of my aunt’s losses. Even though she didn't talk about it openly, the energy in the room shifted whenever it was mentioned. It taught me that pregnancy is a fragile, conditional state. I find myself holding my breath, almost afraid to get too excited, because I’ve seen how quickly the script can flip. 

It’s ironic—my mother and aunt were the ultimate "don't complain, just do it" women, and yet, here I am, drowning in the mental load of it all. I keep expecting to hit those same walls they hit, and when I have a good day, I feel guilty or suspicious of it. I want to be able to enjoy this, to feel that glow everyone talks about, but their experiences left me with a blueprint that says, "Be careful, don't assume anything, and keep your guard up." I’m trying to rewrite that, but old patterns are stubborn things.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

It was a specific month—about four months into us trying—where I had a chemical pregnancy. I knew exactly what was happening because, well, I’m a nurse. I saw the faint line, then the steady decline in my HCG levels before I even missed a period. It was so clinical, so precise, and yet it felt like a wrecking ball hit my chest.

I didn't tell anyone, not at first. I felt an immense, irrational shame, as if my body had failed its own biological assessment. I sat in our bathroom, looking at the test, and I felt like a fraud. How could I, who spends her life guiding parents through the miracle of development, not even manage a successful implantation? 

I eventually told Mark, and that was the moment our relationship shifted from "trying" to "enduring." He didn't try to fix it. He didn't give me medical reassurance. He just held me on the bathroom floor for a long time. That was a turning point. We stopped talking about the mechanics of the process and started talking about the emotional stakes. My colleagues at work had no idea, and that was the loneliest part—walking into the clinic, smiling, and performing my duties while feeling completely hollowed out. That secret, the silent grief, made me realize how much of this journey is performed in the dark, away from the eyes of the people who think they know me.
Who else was involved in supporting you — or perhaps adding to the pressure — at that time?

There wasn’t really anyone else. I kept it very locked down. In some ways, I think I protected my mother and sister from knowing, because if I told them, it would make it "real" in a way I couldn't handle yet. I didn't want the pity or the "you're still young, try again" platitudes. 

But I’ll be honest, the pressure came from my own expectations. I’m surrounded by parents every day who are so desperate for answers, and there I was, silently wrestling with the fact that I couldn't navigate my own cycle. Every time a colleague would ask, "So, are you and Mark done with one, or thinking about another?" I felt a sharp, internal sting. It felt like they were prodding at a wound I was trying to keep bandaged. 

If anything, my professional environment added a layer of pressure to be "fine." I felt like I had to be the steady, capable nurse at work to balance out the erratic, emotional version of myself I was hiding at home. It was exhausting. I felt like I was wearing a costume. When I look back at that time, I realize how much I pushed everyone away. I held it all in because I was terrified that if I let it out, I’d never be able to stop crying.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

My midwife is currently the most important person in this landscape, though it’s a delicate balance. I chose her because I wanted a more holistic, supportive approach than what I saw in the high-risk OB’s office, but I admit I’m a difficult patient. I walk in with my list of questions about protein levels and genetic markers, and she has to gently nudge me back from the ledge of my own professional brain.

She’s important because she’s the one trying to get me to actually *be* a patient. When I start getting into the weeds of medical terminology, she’ll sometimes just pause, look at me, and ask, "Heike, how are *you* doing today, not your chart?" That pause is everything. It’s infuriating and exactly what I need.

The OB I saw for that initial consult felt like a cold shower; he was purely about the risks of my age. But my midwife? She acknowledges the data—she knows the stakes as well as I do—but she frames it within the context of me as a person who wants this baby. That relationship is my anchor. I don't feel like a case study with her. I feel like someone she’s actually rooting for, which is a feeling I haven't really allowed myself to have until now. It’s a work in progress, letting someone else hold the medical reins.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

Mark tries to be there for every appointment that he possibly can, though with the toddler’s schedule and his own work, it isn't always feasible. When he is there, his presence changes the entire frequency of the room. He’s the one who forces me to actually *listen* to the midwife instead of mentally critiquing her advice or analyzing the blood pressure cuff calibration. 

There’s this one memory from a scan a couple of weeks ago. I was staring at the monitor, my mind cataloging everything—the fetal heart rate, the size, the anatomy—trying to find a flaw. Mark just reached over, took my hand, and squeezed it, grounding me back in the room. He whispered, "Look at the baby, Heike, not the pixels." That shift in focus is what he brings. He’s my buffer against my own clinical cynicism.

In our day-to-day, he’s the one who insists I sit down when the bone-deep exhaustion hits. He doesn't treat me like I’m "geriatric" or fragile, but he’s remarkably gentle about the toll this is taking. His involvement isn't just about showing up to appointments; it’s about pulling me out of my head. Without him, I think I would have turned this pregnancy into a full-time, high-stress research project. He keeps it a human experience.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

It’s been both, honestly. On the surface, it’s brought us closer because we’re constantly navigating these little hurdles together—the exhaustion, the toddler’s needs, the appointments. There’s a quiet, shared intimacy in that. But there’s definitely tension, too. Sometimes I find myself resenting that he *can* just be the partner, while I’m the one carrying the literal and clinical weight of it. 

I think I sometimes snap at him when he tries to be "too" optimistic, like when he says, "Everything will be fine," and I immediately feel the urge to correct him with a litany of potential complications. I can see the frustration in his eyes then—he feels like he’s walking on eggshells, and I feel like I’m being patronized. It’s hard for him to watch me struggle with my own brain. 

But then there are those moments, like on the porch in the evening, where we stop trying to "solve" the pregnancy. We just sit there, maybe holding hands, looking at the trees, and we’re just us again. It’s a work in progress. I think we’re both learning how to be parents to a new baby while remembering that we still need to be partners to each other. It’s not always graceful, but we’re holding steady.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

Honestly, I’ve had to be very careful with where I take my worries. Early on, I made the mistake of bringing my "data-mining" habits into online forums, and that was disastrous. I’d be scrolling through threads about complications at 3:00 AM, and it just validated every clinical fear I had. It felt like I was crowdsourcing disaster stories. Now, I’ve pulled back completely from that. 

I’ve mostly kept my circle small. I lean on that one friend I mentioned, the one who had her daughter at forty-one. She’s my filter. When I have a worry that feels "clinical," I’ll text her, and she’s the one who reminds me that feeling a bit off—or even just panicked—is part of the human experience, not necessarily a diagnostic red flag. She’s a buffer between my nurse brain and my pregnant reality.

My midwife is the formal anchor, of course, but these personal relationships work alongside her by filling in the gaps where medical advice can’t reach. My midwife gives me the physiological perspective, but my friend gives me the *emotional* permission to be scared, to be tired, and to be uncertain without feeling like I’m failing. It’s a delicate balance. I’m finally realizing I don't need a medical solution for every single anxiety; sometimes, I just need a human being to tell me that I’m going to make it through to the other side.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

There was actually this one woman in my prenatal yoga class—a complete stranger initially. I went because I thought the stretching might help with the pelvic pain, but I was so braced for it to be one of those overly saccharine spaces. But this woman, she’s older, maybe even a little older than me, and she’s just… blunt. She doesn't have the glossy, filtered approach that makes me want to scream.

One night, after a particularly grueling session where I was clearly struggling to catch my breath, she just leaned over and whispered, "It’s okay to hate parts of this, you know. You don't have to love every second to love the baby." 

It caught me so off-guard that I actually teared up. I’d spent all this time trying to be the "perfect" nurse-patient, holding my breath and maintaining this facade of professional competence, and she just cut right through it. We started grabbing tea afterward, and it’s become this strange, quiet ritual. She’s not a medical peer, she’s not family, but she’s the one I can say the "ugly" things to—the fears about my age, the exhaustion, the guilt. It’s been such an unexpected lifeline, just having a space where I don't have to be anything other than a tired, worried, hopeful woman.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

The NIPT—the non-invasive prenatal testing—was the hurdle. I spent days oscillating between "I need to know everything" and "I can’t bear the weight of what I might find." My nurse brain kept clicking through the likelihood ratios and the false-positive probabilities like a calculator gone haywire. 

I brought the conversation to my midwife, and I went in armed with a folder of research. I felt like a colleague sitting across from her, trying to force a purely intellectual debate. She was so patient, but she eventually set my folder aside and asked, "Heike, what would a 'positive' result mean for you, and what would a 'negative' one actually change about your anxiety right now?" It was the question I hadn't asked myself. 

Mark, bless him, was much simpler about it. He just looked at me and said, "We’re going to love this baby regardless of what that test says. If the test helps *you* sleep, we do it. If it’s just going to make you search for more things to worry about, let’s skip it." 

Hearing him say that—that it was about *my* peace of mind, not a clinical necessity—made the decision much clearer. I realized the test wasn't a diagnostic tool for me; it was an emotional shield. We decided to do it, but I leaned on Mark to hold that result until the moment we were ready to look. We treated it as a choice, not an obligation.
How did that affect your sense of being in control of your own care — did you feel like the decision was truly yours?

It’s a strange thing, feeling like you’re "in control" when your body is doing something as inherently unpredictable as growing a human at forty-two. For the longest time, I thought control meant knowing every possible outcome, every statistic, and every risk factor. I thought if I just had enough data, I could prevent the "what-ifs" from becoming real. But that wasn't control—that was just an exhausting, constant state of hyper-vigilance.

When I chose to lean into Mark and my midwife rather than just my medical textbooks, I finally felt like I was actually in the driver’s seat. It wasn't about the *result* of the test, but the *process* of how we handled it. Realizing that I had the authority to say, "I need to do this for my own peace of mind," or, "I am not ready to process this right now," felt like reclaiming a piece of myself.

It’s ironic. As a nurse, I’m used to being the one who guides the care plan, but here, I had to let go of the "nurse brain" to realize that my care wasn't just about clinical protocols—it was about my emotional autonomy. It was mine. Deciding to put the data aside and trust in the support I had around me... that was the first time I felt like I wasn't just a medium-risk pregnancy, but a woman actually making her own way through this.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

My work environment is the biggest pressure cooker, honestly. There is an unspoken expectation of "clinical stoicism"—the idea that because I’m a nurse, I’m somehow immune to the morning sickness or the bone-deep fatigue that knocks me flat at 6:00 PM. I find myself hiding in the supply closet just to take a few deep breaths, terrified that if my coworkers see me struggling, they’ll treat me like a "patient" rather than a peer. It’s a strange, lonely kind of friction. I want to keep contributing, but my body is screaming at me to slow down.

Then there’s my neighborhood in Melrose. It’s such a lovely, family-oriented place, but that can be a double-edged sword. Every time I see a new mom pushing a stroller while I’m navigating my toddler’s tantrums and my own pregnancy exhaustion, I feel a weird, prickling sense of "am I behind?" or "am I doing this too late?" 

I don’t really turn to a faith community, so I’ve had to build my own "sanctuary." My porch has become that—a place where I can watch the trees and just be, away from the clinical gaze of the hospital and the watchful eyes of my neighbors. It’s the only place where I don’t feel like I have to perform or justify my choices. It’s quiet, and for now, that’s enough.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

It’s a peculiar, jarring shift—going from being the one in the white coat, the one who enters a room and commands the space, to being the one on the exam table. Every time I have to get into that gown, I feel this sudden, sharp vulnerability. It’s like all my professional credentials just evaporate, and I’m reduced to my uterus and my age.

Mark is usually the only one there for those moments, and his presence is the only thing that keeps me from spiraling into a purely clinical analysis. When the midwife or the technician is talking, I find myself wanting to jump in—to interpret the sonogram, to discuss the blood pressure readings, to "help" them do their job. It’s a defense mechanism. If I’m the nurse, I’m in control; if I’m the patient, I’m exposed. 

There was one ultrasound recently where I was really struggling, and the tech was being very quiet—standard procedure, really—but my brain instantly started filling the silence with worst-case scenarios. Mark held my hand, and I realized my fingers were white from the grip I had on his. His presence forced me to stay present in *my* body, rather than escaping into the safety of clinical detachment. I’m starting to learn that I don't have to be the doctor in the room. I just have to be me.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

Oh, absolutely. There was this one afternoon when Mark couldn't make it to a check-up because of an emergency at work. I was alone in the exam room, and for the first time in a while, the "nurse" side of me felt completely abandoned. I was staring at the ceiling tiles, counting the little holes, just waiting for the midwife. When she came in, I felt incredibly exposed—not because she wasn't professional, but because there was no one there to hold the space for me as a *person* instead of a patient.

Without Mark, there was no one to remind me that I didn't have to be the expert. I found myself reciting my own blood pressure numbers and asking about potential variations in the fetal heart rate before she’d even had a chance to check them herself. I was performing the role of "competent, informed patient" just to feel safe, because without my anchor, I felt like a paper boat in a storm. 

When Mark is there, I don't feel the need to posture. His hand in mine feels like a physical barrier between me and the cold, clinical reality of the exam table. Having him there means I don't have to be my own advocate, my own physician, and my own protector all at once. I can just be me, and that makes all the difference in the world.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

Honestly, it feels like I’ve been navigating a maze with a blindfold on, despite working in a hospital. You’d think I’d know the system, but when it’s *your* pregnancy—especially at forty-two—the bureaucracy feels suffocating. My midwife has been the one consistent light. She doesn’t just hand me a stack of forms; she actually stops to explain the *why* behind the scheduling and the testing. She’s the only one who doesn’t treat my need for information like a nuisance.

My husband, Mark, has been my logistical rock. He’s the one who navigates the insurance portal—which, frankly, is a nightmare—and he’s the one who makes sure my appointments don’t clash with the toddler’s naps. Without him, I’d be drowning in paperwork.

The ones who made it harder? It’s the administrative layers. Every time I get a generic, automated email from the hospital system about "advanced maternal age" screenings, it’s like a slap in the face. It feels so dehumanizing. It strips away the nuance of my life and just leaves me as a line item on a risk spreadsheet. I’ve had to fight to keep the "human" element of my care front and center, constantly pushing back against the default settings of a system that really just wants to categorize me and move on to the next patient.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

Beyond my midwife, that friend I mentioned—the one who had her daughter at forty-one—was the only real "translator" I had. When the hospital started firing off these standardized, slightly alarmist messages about my "advanced" status, I’d be ready to call the billing department or the OB's office, completely on edge. She would just laugh, in that knowing, sympathetic way, and tell me, "Heike, ignore the automated noise. That’s just the system covering its own liability, not a reflection of your health."

That was a huge paradigm shift for me. I’m so used to reading medical documentation as objective truth that I hadn't fully processed that these systems are built to prioritize risk mitigation over patient comfort. She helped me realize that the "system" is just a set of checkboxes that don't know me, my history, or my capability. 

I’ve also started talking to a doula who specializes in later-in-life pregnancies. She’s been invaluable in helping me understand what I’m actually entitled to ask for. She’s the one who gave me the language to say, "I’m not interested in this specific screening right now," without feeling like I was being a difficult or non-compliant patient. She’s helped me bridge the gap between being a nurse who knows the rules and a patient who actually has rights.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

I really only trusted the people who didn't immediately lead with my age or my "high-risk" status. Before I was even pregnant, I spoke briefly with a midwife I’d consulted with years ago for a routine check. She was the first person I felt I could rely on because she didn’t treat my desire for a second child as a medical problem to be managed. She listened to my concerns about balancing my work and my home life with a genuine, human interest. 

Trust, for me, comes when someone looks past the clinical profile. In my professional life, I’m constantly evaluating people, but I’ve realized I’m quite guarded when the tables are turned. If someone starts their advice with "Well, at forty-two, the statistics suggest..." they’ve lost me. I know the statistics! I live them every day. 

I trusted my current midwife because she asked me, "What does your ideal birth look like?" instead of "What are you worried about regarding your risks?" That pivot meant everything. It signaled that she saw me as a woman, not a geriatric pregnancy case study. She created a space where my expertise as a nurse was respected, but my vulnerability as a mother-to-be was held. That’s the only kind of relationship I’ve found safe enough to lean into.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Oh, definitely. That was my biggest struggle early on. There was a consult I had with an OB before I was even pregnant, and the conversation felt like a lecture. He spoke in absolutes—"At your age, we'll need to do X, Y, and Z; we'll monitor you closely, and we’ll prepare for these complications." It wasn't a conversation; it was a roadmap he’d already laid out before I’d even sat down.

I felt like my agency was being stripped away, replaced by a pre-determined risk management protocol. I remember sitting there, nodding along like a good student, but internally I was screaming. I felt like a statistic that needed to be "handled" rather than a person making a choice about her family. 

Because I’m a nurse, I think they expect me to just "get it"—to agree with the clinical necessity of it all. It’s almost like they think I don't need the "softer" side of care because I already know the biology. But that made it worse. I felt pushed toward decisions—like genetic screenings or increased surveillance—without anyone asking how I *felt* about the anxiety those procedures would trigger. It felt like I was being herded into a specific lane, and I had to fight just to ask, "Do we have other options?" or "Can we wait on this?" It was exhausting.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

I felt like my body was a piece of fine machinery that I was constantly calibrating, but I also felt like I was running out of time. Before I was pregnant, I looked at my body through a very clinical, almost diagnostic lens. I was aware of every little fluctuation in my cycle, every sign of aging, as if my body were a chart I had to keep "in range." I didn't talk about that openly—to admit you’re watching your own fertility decline feels like admitting you’re losing a race.

With Mark, we talked about the logistics of it, but I didn't really voice the fear that my body might not be up to the task. I think I was afraid that if I said it out loud, it would become a self-fulfilling prophecy. I’d watch other women and feel a mix of envy and "clinical" judgment—wondering if they understood the risks they were taking, or if I was just being overly cynical.

I kept a lot of that distance because I didn't want to be the "worried older woman." I wanted to be the healthy, capable nurse who just... did it. Now, having gone through that early struggle, I realize how much I was distancing myself from my own physical experience. I was treating my body like an instrument, not a home.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

It's embarrassing to admit, but my criteria for "trust" was initially just how clinical the language was. If it had citations, medical jargon, and a detached tone, I labeled it "reliable." I was so desperate for facts—for anything that could give me a sense of control over my own biology—that I completely ignored the emotional toll. I trusted PubMed and medical journals because I thought they were the "truth," but they weren't the truth for a person; they were the truth for a population. They didn't account for the fact that I was already exhausted, stressed, or grieving a loss. 

I’ve learned to be much more skeptical now. I’ve stopped trusting the sites that use scare tactics or push "geriatric pregnancy" outcomes as a way to sell prenatal supplements or specialized testing. Those sources feel like they're preying on exactly the kind of anxiety I was feeding. 

Now, I look for nuance. If a source acknowledges that my experience as a forty-two-year-old mother is unique and doesn't just treat me like a high-risk liability, I’m much more likely to listen. I’ve realized that the most "reliable" information isn't the data itself, but how that information is framed. If I walk away from an article feeling more anxious, I close the tab immediately. That’s my new standard.
Did you ever come across advice or information that felt hard to access — perhaps because of cost, language, or just not knowing where to start?

The biggest barrier I faced wasn't cost or language—it was the wall of "professional courtesy" and assumptions. Because I’m a nurse, people assume I have some sort of internal bypass to the system, that I know exactly who to call or which specialist is the best. It’s isolating. I felt like I couldn't admit I didn't know where to start without feeling like I was failing my own profession.

The information I *really* needed—the stuff about how to handle the emotional weight of being an older mother—was virtually nonexistent in the clinical settings. When I tried to find support for the psychological aspect, I was just handed a list of genetic counselors. It felt like the system was telling me, "We can analyze your baby’s DNA, but we don't have the tools to help you manage the fear."

I felt like I was searching for a language that didn't exist in the hospital hallways. I wanted to talk about the grief of a loss, the fear of age-related complications, and the sheer exhaustion of parenting a toddler while pregnant, but the system only offered me "clinical pathways." Finding a doula who actually understood that dynamic was like finally finding a map for a territory I thought I had to navigate alone. It shouldn't be that hard to find permission to just be human.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

My age was—and still is—the giant neon sign hanging over my head every time I step into a clinic. Before I was pregnant, every time I mentioned even *thinking* about another baby, I felt that shift in the provider’s body language. It was a subtle, almost imperceptible tensing up, like they were bracing for a "difficult" patient or a complicated case. It made me feel like I was already "broken" before I’d even started.

It’s humiliating, honestly. I’ve lived in this body for forty-two years, and suddenly, it’s being reduced to a series of probabilities and "advanced maternal age" risks. I felt like I had to apologize for my own fertility. I’d walk into an exam room and almost feel the need to say, "I know, I know—I’m old." 

It felt like a moral failing, even though I know logically that's ridiculous. The medical system treats age as a pathology to be mitigated, not a stage of life. Every time they brought it up, I felt like my autonomy was being chipped away. I wasn't a woman wanting to grow my family; I was a geriatric patient with a high probability of needing extra surveillance. It made me want to hide, to avoid the doctor, to try and "out-perform" my own biology just so I wouldn't have to hear them tell me I was a risk.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

Mark was involved, but he had to learn how to walk the line between being a partner and being a sounding board for my professional anxieties. Before we officially started trying, there were evenings where I’d be obsessing over my own cycle tracking, my hormone levels, and what the "optimal" stats looked like on paper. He’d listen to it all, but he was the one who had to gently pull me back from the edge.

He’d ask me, "Do you want to talk about the data, or do you want to talk about how we’re feeling?" It’s a simple distinction, but it was massive. He didn’t just show up to appointments; he showed up to the *process*. 

He didn't really have a say in the "clinical" side of things—he’s not a medical professional, and he trusts my judgment in that arena—but he was the one who insisted we didn't turn our bedroom into a clinical lab. He kept the "trying" phase from becoming entirely transactional. If I hadn’t had him there to pull me back, I would have spent that entire time living inside a spreadsheet, looking for problems that didn't exist yet. He was my sanity, the only one who didn't look at me like a textbook. He just looked at me like his wife.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

Yes, Mark has been to most of my major appointments so far, and honestly, he changes the entire gravity of the room. When I go alone, I feel this intense pressure to hold up my end of the "medical conversation"—to prove I understand the tests, to show I'm on top of the risks, to be the "nurse" who isn't intimidated by the clinical jargon. I end up performing competence instead of expressing my own fears.

When Mark is there, the dynamic shifts. He isn't a medical professional, so he doesn't try to speak the language, which actually gives *me* permission to stop doing it, too. He asks the questions that aren't about protein levels or genetic markers, but about me—like, "How is she supposed to manage this exhaustion?" or "Is there a way to do this with less stress?" 

He acts as a buffer. If a doctor starts leaning too hard into the "advanced maternal age" fear-mongering, Mark is the one who gently redirects. He’s my permission slip to be a person, not just a patient. With him there, I feel less like a case study and more like a human being on a journey. He’s the reason I’m starting to trust my own voice again, rather than just repeating the one I learned in nursing school.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

The moments where my voice mattered were few, but distinct. I remember a consultation with a nurse practitioner before I officially started trying—she didn’t pull up my chart immediately. She looked at me, asked how I was feeling about the whole prospect, and *listened*. When I expressed fear about my age, she didn't jump to the risk statistics; she acknowledged the validity of my anxiety. In that moment, I felt like a human being, not a case file. My voice felt like it was part of the conversation, not just background noise to a clinical checklist.

Then there were the other moments—the majority, honestly—where my voice felt like it didn't exist at all. I recall an interaction with a specialist who treated my questions about NIPT with a sort of impatient indulgence. I’d ask about the false-positive rates, and he’d essentially dismiss me with a, "Don't worry, we'll handle the screening, you just need to show up." It was as if my nursing background made my questions annoying—like I was overstepping—but my status as a "geriatric" patient made my concerns invalid. It was infuriating. I felt like I was being spoken *at*, not *to*. It left me feeling silenced, as if the only way to be a "good patient" was to surrender my autonomy and stop thinking for myself.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

It’s been a slow, sometimes painful evolution. Early on, I thought "being in charge" meant proving I was the smartest person in the room. I wanted to use my nursing background to negotiate every intervention like it was a clinical strategy. But I’ve realized that was just another way of trying to control the uncontrollable.

As the weeks have gone on, my definition of "in charge" has shifted. Now, it means having the courage to say, "I’m not looking at that data right now," or "I need you to slow down and explain this to me as a person, not a professional." It’s about setting boundaries that protect my peace, even if the medical system finds those boundaries inconvenient.

I’ve stopped feeling guilty about pushing back. When a practitioner starts reciting the risks of my age for the tenth time, I actually have the confidence now to say, "I’m fully aware of the statistics; what I need to discuss is how we manage my well-being today." That’s a huge shift for me. I’m starting to trust that my intuition—the part of me that isn't a nurse, just a mother—is a legitimate piece of my care plan. Being in charge isn't about knowing the answers; it’s about having the authority to decide which questions actually matter to me and my baby.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

The moments I felt truly respected were the rare ones where a provider stopped looking at my chart and started looking at me. There was a time during an early scan where I was spiraling about the "what-ifs," and the technician actually stopped, turned off the machine, and just let me vent. She didn’t offer a clinical "it’s likely fine," or a "statistically speaking." She just said, "That sounds incredibly heavy to carry alone. What do you need right now?" That single question shifted my entire experience of that appointment. I felt like an expert on my own emotional landscape, and she gave me the space to navigate it without judgment.

Conversely, there have been so many moments where I felt completely erased. It’s almost always when I try to bring my professional background into the room. If I mention, "I’ve seen this progression in my clinic," or "I’m aware of the research on this," the provider’s eyes often glaze over or they become defensive. It’s like they view my knowledge as an impediment rather than a bridge. They’ll dismiss my concerns with a "Don't worry your head about that," or "Just leave the medicine to us." It’s incredibly belittling. Being treated like a nuisance just because I want to be an active participant in my own care is a form of silencing that I’ve grown to loathe.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

Mark has become my "gatekeeper" in the best way possible. When we’re in an appointment and a practitioner starts pushing a routine, high-risk intervention that I’m not sure I’m comfortable with, Mark is the one who calmly asks, "Is there a reason we *must* do that today, or can we give her some time to think?" It changes the power dynamic entirely. I’m the one who feels the pressure to be the "good patient" because I’m a nurse, so having him step in and play the "concerned partner" role gives me the breathing room I need to advocate for myself.

I feel incredibly relieved by his involvement, though I’ve had to learn to let go of the need to manage every detail myself. It’s a bit of a dance—me trying to relinquish the reins without feeling like I’m losing control. But his involvement has made it feel less like I’m walking through a minefield alone. 

It’s actually brought us closer, sharing the burden of these choices. We aren’t just "managing" a pregnancy; we’re figuring out what we want for this baby and for our family. It’s moved from a clinical to-do list to a shared vision, and honestly, having his support has made the "high-risk" label feel so much less heavy. I finally feel like I’m not just a patient; I’m a person with a partner who has my back.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It’s been a tug-of-war. Some days, I feel a strange, primal pride in what my body is doing—growing a life at forty-two feels like a quiet rebellion against time. But most days? I feel like a stranger in my own skin. The nausea, the metallic taste, the exhaustion… it’s like my body has been requisitioned for a project I’m not fully in charge of. As a nurse, I’m used to my body being a tool, something that shows up and functions perfectly. Now, it’s unpredictable, and that is terrifying. 

I’ve had to grapple with a lot of "imposter syndrome" regarding my physical state. I feel like I’m supposed to be this glowing, serene mother, but instead, I’m just a tired woman who wants a nap and a break from the anxiety. It’s hard to sit with that gap between the expectation and the reality. 

I don't talk about this with my colleagues; they’d just see it as "symptoms to manage." The only person I’ve felt truly safe with is that woman from my prenatal yoga class. We were grabbing tea, and I finally just admitted that I felt like I’d lost the manual to my own self. She just nodded and said, "You’re not a machine, Heike. You’re a person." That permission to be imperfectly human… it was everything.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

It's the comments from healthcare professionals that stick the most—the ones where they look at my chart and then look at me with this sort of pitying, professional concern. One OB told me, "Well, at your age, your body is really doing a lot of heavy lifting," which was meant to be empathetic but felt like a confirmation that I was fundamentally *too old* for this. It made me feel like my body was a piece of equipment that was running at the very edge of its warranty. It's hard not to internalize that as "you're failing" or "you're barely holding it together."

Then there's the unsolicited advice from strangers, which is its own special kind of exhausting. When I’m at the clinic with my toddler and someone catches sight of my bump and says, "Oh, you're so brave," it sounds like a backhanded compliment. It feels like they’re saying, "I see you’re playing a dangerous game with your body." 

Mark, thankfully, is the complete opposite. He’s the only one who looks at my changing body and just sees the baby. He’ll put his hand on my stomach and tell me I look beautiful, and for a second, I can actually believe him. He’s the only voice that cuts through the clinical noise of "advanced maternal age" and reminds me that this is a life, not just a liability.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, as we’ve been talking, I’ve realized how much I’ve been holding onto this need to "solve" the pregnancy, like it’s a clinical case I’m responsible for curing. I think that’s the hardest part of being a healthcare professional who is also a patient—the assumption that you can intellectualize your way out of the vulnerability. 

There’s this unspoken weight on people like me to be the "perfect patient"—the one who understands the risks, follows the protocols, and stays stoic. But underneath all that professional armor, I’m just a woman who is tired, scared, and deeply, desperately hopeful that this baby arrives safely. I think maternity care—not just for me, but for everyone—needs to hold space for that. It shouldn't be just about checking boxes or managing risk; it should be about acknowledging that we are human beings experiencing the most profound, unpredictable transition of our lives.

I’m leaving here feeling a little less like a "geriatric" risk factor and more like a woman who is allowed to be messy, tired, and uncertain. I hope that’s what your research captures—the real, human messy truth, not just the clinical version. Thank you for just… listening. It’s actually made me feel a bit lighter than I’ve felt in weeks.
